rows.claim_text: The Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority (APCHA) was created in 1982 by an Intergovernmental Agreement between the City of Aspen and Pitkin County. APCHA is an independent multi-jurisdictional housing authority charged with overseeing affordable housing and has powers to acquire and dispose of property, plan, construct and manage affordable housing, contract, hire, and raise revenues.
rows.source_label: APCHA History — Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority

rows.claim_text: APCHA regulations define rental and ownership units in Categories 1 through 5 and Resident Occupied (RO). In most cases, deed-restricted homes appreciate at the lesser of 3% per year or CPI, plus any appreciation allowed by the applicable deed restriction.
rows.source_label: APCHA Regulations — current edition (categories, appreciation caps)

rows.claim_text: A CAST / NWCCOG inventory found that of Aspen's 3,278 full-time-occupied units, roughly 70% (2,303) are deed-restricted, the second-highest share among 38 Colorado mountain-resort communities surveyed plus 5 out-of-state ski towns.
rows.source_label: More than two-thirds of Aspen's occupied homes are deed-restricted — Aspen Times (CAST / NWCCOG inventory)

rows.claim_text: Aspen voters approved a 1.0% RETT dedicated to addressing the local housing shortage, effective July 1, 1989. The housing RETT runs alongside a separate 0.5% RETT for the Wheeler Opera House and arts, and transfer taxes are paid by the purchasing party at closing.
rows.source_label: Real Estate Transfer Taxes — City of Aspen Finance Department

rows.claim_text: On November 5, 2024, Aspen voters approved Issue 2A by 66.00% to 34.00%, extending the existing 1.0% real estate transfer tax and 0.45% sales tax that fund affordable housing and transportation initiatives.
rows.source_label: Issue 2A / 2B / 2C results — Aspen Times (Nov 2024)

rows.claim_text: RETT collections for calendar year 2024 totaled approximately $23.9M on 638 free-market transactions against a $19.4M budget; $15.8M of that amount was allocated to housing initiatives.
rows.source_label: Real Estate Transfer Taxes generated $24M for Aspen in 2024 — Aspen Daily News

rows.claim_text: Lumberyard is an 11-acre, 277-unit, 100% affordable rental development near the Airport Business Center along Highway 82, planned as three four-story buildings serving households from below 50% AMI up to 240% AMI. The City selected Gorman & Company as developer in July 2025 and approved financial closing 'A' by Resolution #042, Series 2026, on March 24, 2026.
rows.source_label: Lumberyard Affordable Housing — Aspen Community Voice project page

rows.claim_text: Lumberyard is an 11-acre, 277-unit, 100% affordable rental development near the Airport Business Center along Highway 82, planned as three four-story buildings serving households from below 50% AMI up to 240% AMI. The City selected Gorman & Company as developer in July 2025 and approved financial closing 'A' by Resolution #042, Series 2026, on March 24, 2026.
rows.source_label: Aspen announces start of construction for Lumberyard affordable housing project — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Construction of the first 104 units and related infrastructure began in 2026, with occupancy planned for 2028. Financial closing 'B' is planned for May 2027 for two additional buildings totaling 173 units, with occupancy planned for late 2028 and 2029. The city plans to invest $250M from its housing fund to deliver the full 277-unit buildout.
rows.source_label: Lumberyard Affordable Housing — Aspen Community Voice project page

Financial closing 'B' is planned for May 2027 for two additional buildings totaling 173 units, with occupancy planned for late 2028 and 2029. The city plans to invest $250M from its housing fund to deliver the full 277-unit buildout.
rows.source_label: Aspen announces start of construction for Lumberyard affordable housing project — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: On November 4, 2025, Aspen voters approved Issue 2B by 61.92% to 38.08%, authorizing up to $70M in revenue-backed debt with a total repayment cap of $152.55M for Lumberyard. The debt is to be repaid from the existing affordable-housing RETT, with no new taxes.
rows.source_label: Election 2025: Aspen voters support Lumberyard debt question (Issue 2B) — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: On November 4, 2025, Aspen voters approved Issue 2B by 61.92% to 38.08%, authorizing up to $70M in revenue-backed debt with a total repayment cap of $152.55M for Lumberyard. The debt is to be repaid from the existing affordable-housing RETT, with no new taxes.
rows.source_label: Aspen Nov 2025 TABOR Notice (Issue 2B $70M / $152.55M cap)

rows.claim_text: APCHA's LIHTC inventory is a narrow slice of the local affordable-housing system: 127 Low-Income Housing Tax Credit units, consisting of 40 units at Aspen Country Inn and 87 units at Truscott Phase II.
rows.source_label: APCHA Low-Income Housing Tax Credit inventory memo (Jan 20, 2020)

rows.claim_text: In 2010 the Aspen City Council adopted an affordable-housing-credit program that gives developers a fourth mitigation option. Developers that voluntarily create deed-restricted housing receive certificates that can be sold on the free market and used to offset future housing requirements in other projects.
rows.source_label: City of Aspen Affordable Housing Credit Program — APA Merit Award documentation

rows.claim_text: The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC) was established in 2022 as a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit serving the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys from Aspen to Parachute. Its membership list includes the City of Aspen, and its active programs include Good Deeds.
rows.source_label: West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition — official site

rows.claim_text: The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC) was established in 2022 as a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit serving the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys from Aspen to Parachute. Its membership list includes the City of Aspen, and its active programs include Good Deeds.
rows.source_label: WMRHC About — incorporation history and member list

rows.claim_text: Aspen City Council approved a $1M contribution to WMRHC's Good Deeds program in July 2025, following a $450,000 Aspen contribution in 2024. Regional public funding in the program's inaugural year totaled $2M across participating jurisdictions.
rows.source_label: Aspen allocates $1 million to housing coalition — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Aspen City Council approved a $1M contribution to WMRHC's Good Deeds program in July 2025, following a $450,000 Aspen contribution in 2024. Regional public funding in the program's inaugural year totaled $2M across participating jurisdictions.
rows.source_label: West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition secures more funding for Good Deeds — Aspen Public Radio

rows.claim_text: WMRHC's Good Deeds program can provide eligible homebuyers financial assistance worth up to 30% of a home's purchase price in exchange for a permanent deed restriction that prohibits short-term rentals and second-home ownership and creates stable, price-capped workforce housing.
rows.source_label: Aspen allocates $1 million to housing coalition — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Aspen's short-term-rental program recognizes three permit types under Ordinance No. 9, Series 2022: Lodging Exempt permits for qualifying lodges and condo-hotels, Owner-Occupied permits limited to 120 rental nights per year, and Classic permits for non-owner-occupied or owner-occupied residential properties. Classic permits are capped in certain residential zones, while Commercial (C-1), Commercial Core (CC), Lodge (L), Commercial Lodge (CL), Lodge Overlay (LP), and Lodge Preservation Overlay (LO) have no STR-C permit limit.
rows.source_label: Short-Term Rentals — City of Aspen (Ordinance No. 9, Series 2022)

rows.claim_text: The county does not directly build housing. The Garfield County Housing Authority (GCHA) is the county-chartered entity that channels housing services; its board of commissioners is appointed by the Board of County Commissioners. Per the GCHA April 2025 report to BoCC, the county-managed portfolio is 240 affordable housing units plus 35 deed-restricted rentals. In 2024 GCHA sold 16 units, provided $77,400 in interest-free down-payment-assistance loans, and paid $4,476,468 to local landlords via HUD voucher payments.
rows.source_label: GCHA presents 2024 report to county commissioners — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: The county does not directly build housing. The Garfield County Housing Authority (GCHA) is the county-chartered entity that channels housing services; its board of commissioners is appointed by the Board of County Commissioners. rows.source_label: Garfield County Housing Authority — official site

rows.claim_text: GCHA's Down Payment Assistance Program loans cover up to 3% of purchase price or $15,000 (whichever is less), interest-free, for deed-restricted purchases.
rows.source_label: 2024 GCHA Down Payment Assistance Brochure (PDF)

rows.claim_text: The BoCC has repeatedly allocated the county's annual Private Activity Bond capacity to CHFA to support single-family mortgage loans. In April 2025 the BoCC unanimously voted to allocate $4.1M of 2025 PAB capacity to CHFA. Per county reporting, since 2020 the mechanism has helped approximately 298 county residents acquire homes.
rows.source_label: County authorizes CHFA to issue $4.1 million in bonds — Garfield County government press release

rows.claim_text: On October 21, 2024 the BoCC voted unanimously to opt in to Colorado Proposition 123 (the Colorado State Affordable Housing Fund), committing to a 3% annual affordable-housing stock increase (approximately 30-40 units per year) and a fast-track approval process. The county was one of 21 of 64 Colorado counties that had not opted in before the vote — late among the cohort.
rows.source_label: County commissioners agree to opt in to Proposition 123 — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: On October 21, 2024 the BoCC voted unanimously to opt in to Colorado Proposition 123 (the Colorado State Affordable Housing Fund), committing to a 3% annual affordable-housing stock increase (approximately 30-40 units per year) and a fast-track approval process. The county was one of 21 of 64 Colorado counties that had not opted in before the vote — late among the cohort.
rows.source_label: Commissioners opt in on Prop 123 — Sopris Sun

rows.claim_text: The county itself is not a member of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC). Glenwood Springs and Carbondale joined as municipal members covering their portions of the county; the I-70 / Colorado River Valley municipalities (New Castle, Silt, Rifle, Parachute) have historically held off. The county's posture is to delegate coalition participation to its members rather than join directly.
rows.source_label: County not quite ready to join regional housing coalition — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley is the dominant ownership-stock builder across the county, delivering more than 110 new homeownership opportunities to the Valley over 2024-2026 through new construction and 'development-neutral' conversions. Three county-located projects anchor the surge.
rows.source_label: Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley — August 2025 project updates

rows.claim_text: Wapiti Commons (Rifle) is a 20-home neighborhood; as of August 2025, 14 homeowners had moved in, with the remaining 6 closing in fall 2025. The Confluence (Glenwood Springs, 6 homes at 8th & Midland) is Habitat's first modular project. The L3 at Green Leaf Lofts conversion in Glenwood Springs is an 88-unit rental-to-deed-restricted-condo conversion at 253 Wulfsohn Road; Habitat closed the purchase in November 2024 and completed conversion in April 2025, with sale prices from $320,000 (studio) to $388,000 (1-BR), 80-120% AMI target, and mortgages as low as 2.5%.
rows.source_label: Habitat closes L3 apartment complex sale — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.source_label: L3 condominium conversion project page — Habitat Roaring Fork

rows.claim_text: Rifle Apartments (60 units) received a 9% LIHTC reservation in CHFA's 2023 Round One — the first family LIHTC built in Rifle in roughly 20 years per the CHFA narrative; developer Evergreen Real Estate Group, opened April 2026 with 14 project-based vouchers from GCHA at or below 30% AMI.
rows.source_label: CHFA 2023 Round One Narratives — Rifle Apartments narrative (PDF)

rows.claim_text: Aster Place (Parachute / Battlement Mesa, 58 units, 30-80% AMI on 6.38 acres along Five Stone Quarry Road) received a 9% LIHTC reservation in CHFA's 2024 Round One — the first affordable family LIHTC in the Parachute / Battlement Mesa Special District. GCHA serves as Special Limited Partner with 8 project-based vouchers projected.
rows.source_label: CHFA 2024 Round One Narratives — Aster Place (PDF)

rows.claim_text: Aster Place (Parachute / Battlement Mesa, 58 units, 30-80% AMI on 6.38 acres along Five Stone Quarry Road) received a 9% LIHTC reservation in CHFA's 2024 Round One — the first affordable family LIHTC in the Parachute / Battlement Mesa Special District. GCHA serves as Special Limited Partner with 8 project-based vouchers projected.
rows.source_label: Aster Place project one step closer to offering affordable housing in Parachute — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: Glenwood Gardens / Canyon Vista (Glenwood Springs, 80 units at 20%-80% AMI on 51993 US Highway 6) received federal 4% LIHTC plus state Housing Tax Credits in CHFA's 2024 Round Two announcement; developer Cohen-Esrey Development Group. The BoCC voted 2-1 (Commissioners Jankovsky and Samson in favor; Commissioner Martin opposed) to allocate $3.9M of 2024 PAB capacity to the project. Twenty project-based vouchers are projected from GCHA.
rows.source_label: CHFA Announces 2024 Round Two Housing Tax Credit Awards (Nov 27, 2024)

rows.claim_text: Glenwood Gardens / Canyon Vista (Glenwood Springs, 80 units at 20%-80% AMI on 51993 US Highway 6) received federal 4% LIHTC plus state Housing Tax Credits in CHFA's 2024 Round Two announcement; developer Cohen-Esrey Development Group. rows.claim_text: The Benedict (Glenwood Springs, 34 units 55+ at 2700 Midland Avenue) is a Catholic Charities Housing senior LIHTC project (developer Archdiocesan Housing) — completion September 2025; 7 project-based vouchers from GCHA in 2025.
rows.source_label: Council approves senior affordable housing project (The Benedict) — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: The county is Colorado's leading natural-gas producer with 11,000+ producing wells; county government and public services rely heavily on property-tax and oil-and-gas-tax revenue. The county maintains roughly $120M in cash reserves including a roughly $21M dedicated energy-mitigation fund (federal mineral-lease revenue plus state oil-and-gas tax distributions) earmarked for O&G-driven impacts.
rows.source_label: Oil and gas — Garfield County government landing

rows.claim_text: The county is Colorado's leading natural-gas producer with 11,000+ producing wells; county government and public services rely heavily on property-tax and oil-and-gas-tax revenue. The county maintains roughly $120M in cash reserves including a roughly $21M dedicated energy-mitigation fund (federal mineral-lease revenue plus state oil-and-gas tax distributions) earmarked for O&G-driven impacts.
rows.source_label: Oil and gas industry has the county flush with cash — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Demand patterns are bifurcated: the eastern half (Glenwood Springs, Carbondale) is shaped by upper-valley commuter spillover from the Roaring Fork resort labor market; the western half (Rifle, Silt, Parachute, Battlement Mesa) is shaped by the natural-gas workforce. Stanford's 'And the West' analysis frames the county as 'astride two Wests.'
rows.source_label: Astride two Wests: Colorado county faces a tricky economic balance — Stanford 'And the West'

rows.claim_text: In 2025 the county implemented a hiring freeze over a projected $9M shortfall driven by an O&G revenue dip and drew approximately $10.5M from reserves. The adopted 2026 budget — $118.7M operating, $11.1M capital, $6.7M discretionary — frames housing affordability in policy directives as a 'land use + partnership + Proposition 123' obligation rather than direct capital commitment.
rows.source_label: County adopts 2026 budget after challenging process — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.source_label: County commissioners approve 2026 policy directives — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: APCHA was created in 1982 by an Intergovernmental Agreement between the City of Aspen and Pitkin County as an independent multi-jurisdictional housing authority. APCHA describes itself as the oldest and largest mountain-resort workforce-housing program in North America.
rows.source_label: APCHA History — Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority

rows.claim_text: APCHA now has an eight-member Board of Directors, with five voting members and three alternates. Aspen City Council and the Pitkin County Board of County Commissioners each hold one voting seat and one alternate, while the other four board members are appointed jointly. The county board page describes APCHA powers including investigating housing needs, issuing bonds, borrowing funds, securing mortgages, managing employee housing projects, and adopting annual rental and ownership qualifications.
rows.source_label: APCHA Board of Directors

rows.claim_text: APCHA now has an eight-member Board of Directors, with five voting members and three alternates. Aspen City Council and the Pitkin County Board of County Commissioners each hold one voting seat and one alternate, while the other four board members are appointed jointly. The county board page describes APCHA powers including investigating housing needs, issuing bonds, borrowing funds, securing mortgages, managing employee housing projects, and adopting annual rental and ownership qualifications.
rows.source_label: Housing Authority Board — County government

rows.claim_text: APCHA oversees 1,652 deed-restricted ownership units and about 1,430 rental units. Ownership and rental units are organized in Categories 1 through 5 plus Resident Occupied (RO); Categories 6 and 7 have been rolled into Category 5. Deed restrictions typically cap appreciation at 3% per year or CPI, whichever is less, with approved capital improvements allowed under APCHA rules.
rows.source_label: Inventory shows who lives in APCHA deed-restricted ownership housing — Aspen Journalism

rows.claim_text: Pitkin County placed Issue 1A on the November 5, 2024 ballot to authorize a 1.5-mill ad valorem property tax for up to 25 years for affordable and attainable housing. Aspen Daily News reported the worker-housing 1A question was winning on election night, and later Aspen Times coverage states voters approved the mill levy in 2024. The county projected approximately $8.5M in annual revenue, with an estimated cost of about $121 per $1M of residential value and $435 per $1M of commercial value.
rows.source_label: Issue 1A BoCC Resolution — County government PDF

rows.source_label: How much money the housing mill levy raised in 2025 — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: The county Employee Housing Impact Fee, last amended by BOCC Ordinance 03-2020 in February 2020, is recalculated annually using methodology tied to updated data points such as HUD median-income data, residential sales prices, and mortgage interest rates.
rows.source_label: Housing Impact Fee BoCC AIS — County government PDF

rows.claim_text: Pitkin County requires short-term rentals in unincorporated areas to have a valid license. County rules include a four-night minimum, 120-night maximum, proof of short-term-rental activity between May 11, 2017 and May 11, 2022, and a tiered fee structure of 0.05%, 0.06%, or 0.07% of assessed home market value based on rental-night tier.
rows.source_label: Short-Term Rentals — Pitkin County government landing page

rows.claim_text: The Pitkin County Board of County Commissioners voted 3-1 on June 14, 2023 to prohibit STRs in the Rural Remote zone. The county ordinance text states that no short-term-rental license shall issue for any property in the Rural and Remote zone district.
rows.source_label: Short-term rentals banned in the most remote parts of the county — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: On December 28, 2021, at a special meeting, the Carbondale Board of Trustees accepted a gift of 14 vacant parcels in the Town Center development, located across Colorado Avenue from Carbondale Town Hall and the recreation center. Per the county assessor's records as reported by the Post Independent, the combined assessed valuation (not market value) for the parcels came to just under $2.4 million.
rows.source_label: Carbondale to assess possibilities after $2.4M land donation to town — Glenwood Springs Post Independent (Feb 8, 2022)

rows.claim_text: The Town selected Artspace Projects, Inc. as the original master developer for the Town Center site, with Artspace operating in Colorado through the state's Space to Create program — Carbondale is the fourth Space to Create community.
rows.source_label: Carbondale Space to Create: Town Center — Artspace project page

rows.claim_text: CHFA's May 30, 2024 Round One award announcement lists 13 awarded projects across Colorado; the Carbondale Town Center proposal is absent from the awardee list, confirming the 9% LIHTC application was denied.
rows.source_label: CHFA Announces 2024 Round One Housing Tax Credit Awards (May 30, 2024)

rows.claim_text: Per Andrew Michaelson of Artspace, reported by the Sopris Sun on July 24, 2024, the project team returned with CHFA's feedback on the denial and prepared to apply again in February 2025.
rows.source_label: Carbondale Report: Board of Adjustments reactivated, pool naming rights reconsidered, Town Center reworked — Sopris Sun (Jul 24, 2024)

rows.claim_text: Artspace's contract on the Town Center project concluded in late 2025. The Town subsequently engaged Robert Schultz Consulting to draft a new Request for Proposals to develop the Town Center parcels into affordable housing.
rows.source_label: Carbondale Report: New pool poised to open May 23 — Sopris Sun (Feb 25, 2026)

rows.claim_text: Carbondale voters passed Ballot Issue 2A on November 8, 2022, enacting a 6% short-term-rental excise tax that took effect January 1, 2023. The Town's Finance Department FAQ confirms the revenue is dedicated primarily to fund 'the promotion, regulation, development and protection of affordable and attainable housing, projects and programs, including capital expenditures.' The measure passed with more than 72% support.
rows.source_label: Short-Term Rental Tax FAQ — Town of Carbondale Finance Department

The measure passed with more than 72% support.
rows.source_label: Carbondale vacation-rentals tax for workforce housing passes easily — Glenwood Springs Post Independent (Nov 2022)

rows.claim_text: In 2023, the Carbondale Board of Trustees adopted the Town's first Community Housing Plan — building, per the Town's own Affordable Housing page, 'off of the prior work with the Housing Guidelines and recent efforts of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition.' On November 12, 2024, the Board approved Ordinance No. 10, Series of 2024, amending Chapter 17 of the Carbondale Municipal Code concerning Inclusionary Residential Housing Requirements.
rows.source_label: Affordable Housing program page — Town of Carbondale (Community Housing Plan 2023, Ordinance 10/2024, GCHA contract)

rows.claim_text: Carbondale contracts with the Garfield County Housing Authority to manage the qualifications, sale, approval of improvements, and other aspects of the Town's deed-restricted Community Housing Program.
rows.source_label: Affordable Housing program page — Town of Carbondale (Community Housing Plan 2023, Ordinance 10/2024, GCHA contract)

rows.claim_text: The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC), established in 2022 as a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit, operates a regional housing program across the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys, from Aspen to Parachute. Member jurisdictions per the coalition's own About page are the City of Glenwood Springs, the Town of Carbondale, the Town of Basalt, the Town of Snowmass Village, the City of Aspen, Eagle County, Pitkin County, the Colorado Mountain Local College District, and the Roaring Fork Transit Authority.
rows.source_label: West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition — program & member overview

rows.claim_text: The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC), established in 2022 as a Colorado 501(c)(3) nonprofit, operates a regional housing program across the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys, from Aspen to Parachute. Member jurisdictions per the coalition's own About page are the City of Glenwood Springs, the Town of Carbondale, the Town of Basalt, the Town of Snowmass Village, the City of Aspen, Eagle County, Pitkin County, the Colorado Mountain Local College District, and the Roaring Fork Transit Authority.
rows.source_label: WMRHC About page — member list

rows.claim_text: Per Resolution No. 8, Series of 2023, the Town of Carbondale commits funding 'up to the sum of One Hundred Thousand Dollars ($100,000.00) to the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition for the specific purpose of administering the Regional Deed Restriction Purchase Program in Carbondale.' (Good Deeds is WMRHC's later public brand for that same buy-down program.)
rows.source_label: Resolution No. 8, Series of 2023 — Town of Carbondale ($100,000 commitment to WMRHC's Regional Deed Restriction Purchase Program)

rows.claim_text: Per Aspen Public Radio reporting on April 22, 2024, the program had drawn the following per-jurisdiction commitments: Pitkin County $2,000,000; City of Aspen $450,000 (budgeted but not yet officially approved at the time of reporting); Town of Snowmass Village $250,000; City of Glenwood Springs $200,000; and Town of Carbondale $100,000.
rows.source_label: West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition's 'Good Deeds' buydown program gains traction and dollars — Aspen Public Radio (Apr 22, 2024)

rows.claim_text: WMRHC's Good Deeds program contributes 30% of the contracted purchase price of a qualifying free-market home toward a permanent price-capped deed restriction recorded with the County Clerk and Recorder. Appreciation is capped at 3% simple interest annually (not compounding). Per the program page, eligible home-price ceilings are $1,500,000 in Pitkin County (including and up-valley of Basalt), $1,200,000 in Garfield County (including and up-valley of Glenwood Springs), and $800,000 in the remainder of Garfield County.
rows.source_label: Good Deeds program page — West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (buy-down structure + price ceilings + appreciation cap)

rows.claim_text: The coalition's stated 2026 goal is to convert 30 free-market homes in the Roaring Fork Valley and beyond into deed-restricted housing, at a projected program cost of roughly $13.5 million (compared with approximately $30 million if the same units were produced through new construction, per the Post Independent).
rows.source_label: Aspen-to-Parachute housing coalition aims to convert 30 free-market homes to deed-restricted — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: On June 25, 2025, by a 5-1 vote, the Carbondale Board of Trustees 'pledged $1 million to a regional effort to purchase two trailer parks, representing 139 units, under a community-ownership model for a total cost of $43 million.' Per the Sopris Sun, the two parks are the Mountain Valley Mobile Home Park (near Carbondale) and the Aspen-Basalt Mobile Home Park; Trustee Chris Hassig publicly challenged the Basalt Town Council to increase its contribution given the latter park's proximity to Basalt town limits.
rows.source_label: Carbondale Report: Trustees commit $1 million to trailer parks, challenging Basalt — Sopris Sun (Jun 25, 2025)

rows.claim_text: CSHA is HUD-listed as public housing agency code CO028, a combined housing authority in Colorado Springs. CSHA’s official site says the Housing Choice Voucher lottery will not open in July because of current funding limits and that HUD’s pause on issuing new Housing Choice Vouchers remains in effect.
rows.source_label: HUD PHA Contact Report for Colorado (CSHA listing CO028)

rows.claim_text: HomeCOS is the city’s affordable and attainable housing blueprint for Mayor John Suthers’ goal to build, preserve, or create purchase opportunities for an average of 1,000 affordable units per year over five years. The plan identifies rental supply, homeownership, and permanent supportive housing for people exiting homelessness as core implementation areas.
rows.source_label: HomeCOS — Housing Our Future landing page

rows.claim_text: Mayor Yemi Mobolade’s 2024 Strategic Doing Framework organized city work around five priorities, including housing solutions. The city’s 2025 Strategic Doing Actions continued those five priorities and reported that 44 of 54 actions set in 2024 were completed, with the remaining 10 rolled into 2025.
rows.source_label: Mayor Yemi releases 2024 Strategic Doing Framework — City of Colorado Springs press release

rows.claim_text: The city’s 2022 HomeCOS update reported 1,703 affordable multifamily units under construction or expected to begin construction, 1,236 units anticipated before 2025, and 1,057 affordable units supported during 2022. The same update says the city and Colorado Springs Utilities launched the Affordable and Attainable Multi-Family Fee Rebate Program in the second quarter of 2022.
rows.source_label: Over 1,000 affordable multi-family units supported in 2022 — City of Colorado Springs

rows.claim_text: CHFA’s 2024 Round One Housing Tax Credit announcement listed Sunrise at Shiloh Mesa in Colorado Springs among the developments receiving award reservations, with Greccio Housing named as developer.
rows.source_label: CHFA Announces 2024 Round One Housing Tax Credit Awards (May 30, 2024)

rows.claim_text: CHFA’s 2024 Round Two announcement said 10 developments would receive federal 4 percent and state Housing Tax Credit reservations, including Jet Wing Flats in Colorado Springs, developed by Commonwealth Development Corporation and Solid Rock Community Development Corporation.
rows.source_label: CHFA Announces 2024 Round Two Housing Tax Credit Awards (Nov 27, 2024)

rows.claim_text: CHFA’s 2026 Round One Housing Tax Credit announcement listed Silver Key Apartments at North Weber in Colorado Springs, developed by Silver Key Senior Services, Inc.
rows.source_label: CHFA 2026 Round One Housing Tax Credit Awards (May 21, 2026)

rows.claim_text: The city’s fee-rebate program rules apply to multifamily rental projects within Colorado Springs and the Colorado Springs Utilities service area with all units reserved for residents under 120% AMI. Eligible rebates include Land Use Review, PLDO, Public Works, and Colorado Springs Utilities charges, and the scoring rubric gives points for deeper 50% AMI affordability and energy or water-conservation measures.
rows.source_label: Affordable & Attainable Multi-Family Rental Housing Fee Rebate Program rules (PDF)

rows.claim_text: Proposition 123 participation requires local governments to commit to increasing affordable housing stock by 3% each year in the first three-year cycle and to create a fast-track approval process. A July 2023 Colorado Sun report said only 14 of 336 eligible local governments had applied at that point, while larger municipalities were expected to begin opting in.
rows.source_label: Prop 123 opt-in process explained — Colorado Sun

rows.claim_text: Greenway Flats is described by Springs Rescue Mission as Colorado Springs’ first permanent supportive housing project: 65 rooms for chronically homeless residents, operated on a Housing First premise, with residents moved in over the summer before the article was published.
rows.source_label: What is Greenway Flats — Springs Rescue Mission

rows.claim_text: On March 18, 2026, the Pikes Peak Continuum of Care board voted to shift oversight to Pikes Peak United Way and the City of Colorado Springs effective July 1. The Gazette reported that ChangeLine had decided in early 2024 to step away and that about $5.06 million in FY 2025-2026 funding was delayed by litigation over federal formula changes.
rows.source_label: Federally funded homelessness agency changes hands — Gazette

rows.claim_text: The 2026 Point-in-Time Count identified 1,413 people experiencing homelessness, down from 1,745 in 2025, and 308 people experiencing unsheltered homelessness, down from 522 in 2025.
rows.source_label: Mayor Yemi responds to 2026 PIT count — City of Colorado Springs

rows.claim_text: Tawi Kaan Apartments at 560 E. Empire Street is Cortez’s first permanent supportive housing development. The three-story building has 42 units, split evenly among one-, two-, and three-bedroom homes, for households making 30% or less of area median income. The project celebrated its grand opening on October 15, 2024, and CHFA helped finance it with a roughly $1.2M tax credit award.
rows.source_label: Affordable Housing with Heart (Tawi Kaan opens) — The Journal

rows.claim_text: The Tawi Kaan partnership includes BlueLine Development, The Piñon Project Family Resource Center, Boston Financial, and the Ute Mountain Ute Tribe. In 2025, the Affordable Housing Tax Credit Coalition recognized Tawi Kaan with the Charles L. Edson Recognition of Housing Tax Credit Excellence in the Native American Housing category.
rows.source_label: AHTCC honors Tawi Kaan Apartments with national award — The Journal

rows.claim_text: Denver's Expanding Housing Affordability ordinance became effective July 1, 2022. The city page says EHA updated the 2017 affordable-housing linkage fee and created new affordable-housing requirements for residential developments of 10 or more dwelling units; the March 2022 policy approach describes the paired mandatory-housing and linkage-fee framework.
rows.source_label: EHA Ordinance and Affordable Housing Fee — City of Denver

rows.claim_text: Denver's Expanding Housing Affordability ordinance became effective July 1, 2022. The city page says EHA updated the 2017 affordable-housing linkage fee and created new affordable-housing requirements for residential developments of 10 or more dwelling units; the March 2022 policy approach describes the paired mandatory-housing and linkage-fee framework.
rows.source_label: EHA full policy approach (March 2022) — City of Denver PDF

rows.claim_text: The retained EHA on-site requirements are those directly shown by the city page and policy PDF: new residential development of 10 or more units must build affordable units or use an alternative compliance option; baseline requirements include 8% of units at 60% AMI in typical market areas, 10% at 60% AMI in high market areas, 12% averaging 70% AMI in typical market areas, and 15% averaging 70% AMI in high market areas; income-restricted units have a 99-year affordability term.
rows.source_label: EHA Ordinance and Affordable Housing Fee — City of Denver

rows.claim_text: The linkage-fee side applies to development outside the mandatory-affordable-housing trigger, including residential projects of nine or fewer units and nonresidential development. rows.claim_text: Denver's Dedicated Affordable Housing Fund page documents the 2016 fund-creation process, including September 19 final council approval, the two-source revenue structure of a 0.5-mill property tax and development impact fees, an estimated $150 million over the first 10 years, and a 6,000-unit production or preservation target.

rows.claim_text: Denver later added marijuana revenue to the housing stack. FOX31 reported that Mayor Hancock signed an ordinance increasing the local marijuana tax from 3.5% to 5.5% for affordable housing, while Westword later reported that the 2-percentage-point increase produced nearly $38.8 million from late 2018 through 2022 and helped fund HOST land acquisition, unit development, housing assistance, and supportive services.
rows.source_label: New marijuana taxes will fund affordable housing — FOX31 (2018)

rows.claim_text: The City describes its Land Bank as Fort Collins' only long-term affordable-housing tool: it buys sites that lack infrastructure, holds them as surrounding infrastructure is built, and points to Municipal Code Chapter 23, Article XIII as the program authority. The current city page says the city has bought seven Land Bank sites, sold two for affordable housing, and that the newest parcel was acquired in 2025.
rows.source_label: Fort Collins Land Bank — Social Sustainability landing page

rows.claim_text: The city's Affordable Housing page says projects may qualify for fee credits when they provide housing for formerly homeless individuals, people with disabilities, or people earning 30% of AMI or less. The same page and Article 5 state the affordability thresholds for development incentives: rental projects must provide either 10% of units at 60% AMI or 20% at 80% AMI, while for-sale projects must provide either 10% at 80% AMI or 20% at 100% AMI.
rows.source_label: Development incentives — City of Fort Collins Social Sustainability

rows.claim_text: The city also describes an impact-fee delay incentive: impact fees normally due at building permit may be delayed until certificate of occupancy or December 1 of that year, whichever comes first.

rows.claim_text: Colorado's HB24-1007 ended Fort Collins' U+2 framework in 2024. Collegian and CBS Colorado coverage report that Governor Jared Polis signed HB24-1007 on April 15, 2024, that the law took effect July 1, 2024, and that it barred local occupancy limits based on familial relationship. Collegian's July 2024 coverage says Fort Collins City Council then passed Ordinance 081 and Ordinance 082 on second reading by a 5-2 vote to remove occupancy limits from local codes.
rows.source_label: U+2 ended as Governor signs bill banning occupancy limits — Collegian

rows.claim_text: Glenwood Springs' Workforce Housing Fund was created after voters approved Ballot Measure 2C, a 2.5% accommodations tax on overnight stays at hotels, motels, short-term rentals, and similar lodging. City materials forecast about $1.7 million per year from the tax, while the original September 1, 2022 city release said the council referred the question to the November 8, 2022 ballot.
rows.source_label: Workforce Housing Fund / 2C Budget — City of Glenwood Springs

rows.claim_text: Glenwood Springs' Workforce Housing Fund was created after voters approved Ballot Measure 2C, a 2.5% accommodations tax on overnight stays at hotels, motels, short-term rentals, and similar lodging. City materials forecast about $1.7 million per year from the tax, while the original September 1, 2022 city release said the council referred the question to the November 8, 2022 ballot.

rows.claim_text: The fund is restricted to workforce housing: Post Independent reporting says it can be used only for workforce housing development for a person working within city limits or the 81601 ZIP code, that council appropriates the funds, and that expenditures are subject to an annual public audit. The city's advisory-board page says the WHFAB was commissioned by council in 2023 to help manage the fund and advise council on 2C-funded workforce-housing programs.
rows.source_label: Everything you need to know about Ballot Measure 2C — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: Per CHFA's 2023 Round One award narrative, no family-property LIHTC project had been built in Rifle since 2002. Rifle Apartments (60 units; 30 1-BR, 20 2-BR, 10 3-BR at 30%-80% AMI, with 14 project-based vouchers at or below 30% AMI from the county housing authority) is the city's first family LIHTC in roughly 20 years.
rows.source_label: CHFA 2023 Round One Narratives (Rifle Apartments narrative, p. 86+) — PDF

rows.claim_text: Groundbreaking was September 27, 2024. The grand opening was held April 17, 2026, with 95% lease-up at opening.
rows.source_label: Rifle Apartments groundbreaking — Yield Pro

rows.claim_text: Groundbreaking was September 27, 2024. The grand opening was held April 17, 2026, with 95% lease-up at opening.
rows.source_label: Rifle Apartments opens with 60 income-restricted units — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: RHA was established in 1979 to provide affordable rental housing for senior and disabled residents. The agency operates approximately 106 low-income apartments — 60 HUD rental-assistance / Section 8 units and 46 affordable units — with roughly 150 households on the waitlist per recent reporting.
rows.source_label: Rifle Housing Authority — official site

rows.claim_text: Rifle's documented direct financial commitment to the Rifle Apartments project was $600,000 (CHFA narrative §2d). The city also signed a Letter of Support endorsing the project, and supported the developer's HB 22-1304 Infrastructure & Strong Communities funding request to DOLA ($5,000,000) and DOH Gap Funds request ($3,000,000).
rows.source_label: CHFA 2023 Round One Narratives (Rifle Apartments narrative, p. 86+) — PDF

rows.claim_text: CHFA says Salida Ridge Apartments provides 48 affordable rental units for Salida residents with incomes between 30% and 50% of AMI, was developed by Commonwealth Development Corporation, and received a $1,220,000 federal 9% Housing Tax Credit reservation in 2020 plus $1,611,000 in permanent financing in 2021. Ark Valley Voice identified the project as Confluent Park Apartments, reported the 24 one-bedroom / 21 two-bedroom / three three-bedroom mix, and described it as the first LIHTC development in Salida in more than 20 years.
rows.source_label: Salida Ridge Apartments — CHFA customer/partner story

rows.claim_text: Preliminary 2022 election reporting showed Salida Ballot Issue 2A increasing the annual short-term-rental license to $1,000 for affordable housing, passing 55% to 45%, and Ballot Issue 2B increasing the short-term-rental lodging tax from $4.82 per day to $15 for affordable housing, passing 51% to 47%.
rows.source_label: Preliminary Election 2022 results (Salida Ballot 2A / 2B) — Heart of the Rockies Radio

rows.claim_text: In May 2024, Salida City Council voted 4-1 to reduce fee-in-lieu charges from the prior flat $17.40 per square foot rate. The Mountain Mail reported that the new formula lowered fees for smaller projects and that fee-in-lieu collections fund the city affordable-housing fund.
rows.source_label: Salida Council action on FIL — The Mountain Mail

rows.claim_text: Ark Valley Voice describes South Ark as the remaining portion of the original 190-acre Vandaveer Ranch parcel, purchased by the City of Salida in 2004 for water rights. It also reports that the Salida Natural Resource Center Development Corporation was formed in 2009 to handle financing for the Forest Service building and that the roughly 100-acre South Ark parcel has 50 to 60 developable acres after wetlands, springs, drainages, and ditches are considered.
rows.source_label: Competing visions for the South Ark Neighborhood area — Ark Valley Voice

rows.claim_text: In coverage of Ordinance 2023-16, Ark Valley Voice reported that the South Ark master plan would allow up to 400 housing units, with 300 units deed-restricted and 200 units in the affordable category, deed-restricted for at least five years.
rows.source_label: Council considers modifications to Vandaveer Ranch parcels — Ark Valley Voice

rows.claim_text: Chaffee Housing Authority describes itself as a multi-jurisdictional housing authority formed in 2020 by Chaffee County, the City of Salida, and the Town of Buena Vista.
rows.source_label: Chaffee Housing Authority — official site

rows.claim_text: Chaffee Housing Trust separately describes its work as a Community Land Trust model that provides affordable housing and rentals to qualified low-income residents.
rows.source_label: Chaffee Housing Trust — official site

rows.claim_text: Preliminary November 2024 coverage showed Chaffee Housing Authority Ballot Measure 6A, a 0.5% sales tax for CHA housing projects, failing while Ballot Measure 6B, a de-Brucing measure to retain revenue for affordable housing, passed. In November 2025, Chaffee County voters rejected Ballot Measure 1A, which would have raised the county lodging tax from 1.9% to 5.9%, with 58.19% voting no.
rows.source_label: Chaffee County appears to approve 6B but not 6A — Ark Valley Voice

rows.claim_text: CHA's Jane's Place page describes a half-acre mixed-use development near downtown Salida with four buildings and 17 transitional housing units, plus nonprofit community space and a social-enterprise coffee shop. CHA reported approximately $5.7 million secured for construction, including a $1.287 million DOLA award; Ark Valley Voice later reported a December 2025 ribbon cutting, 13 affordable-housing units plus four Molly House units, and a $1.3 million OEDIT community-revitalization grant.
rows.source_label: Jane's Place — Chaffee Housing Authority project page

rows.claim_text: Ark Valley Voice reported that Salida City Council unanimously approved the Flour Mill Planned Development at 6907 CR 105 in January 2024. The two-acre parcel owned by Rob Gartzman is planned for 69 housing units in a mix of single-family homes, duplexes, townhouses, and apartment buildings; Gartzman said 50% of units would be deed-restricted and priced at 120% AMI, with some at 100% AMI.
rows.source_label: Council gives green light to Flour Mill development — Ark Valley Voice

rows.claim_text: KRDO reported on December 31, 2025, that the Chaffee Housing Authority would receive $1.1 million for 20 apartments near downtown Salida.
